3: bᵉgêʾ ben-hinnōm — 'in the valley of the son of Hinnom' (2 Chronicles 28:3). The specific geographic designation marks the historical site of child-sacrifice under Ahaz and later Manasseh. The toponym gêʾ-hinnōm (valley of Hinnom) — in Greek Gehinnom, contracted to Gehenna — becomes in Second Temple Judaism the name for the place of eschatological punishment. The fire-child rites (hᵉʿbîr bāʾēš, 'passed through the fire') are described in 2 Kings 16:3 and condemned in the Torah (Deuteronomy 18:10; Leviticus 18:21). The Chronicler's naming of the site with the same precision found in Jeremiah's Topheth passages (Jeremiah 7:31-32; 2 Chronicles 19:2-6) links this historical record to the prophetic oracle that the valley would become a 'valley of slaughter.'
9: ûnāḇîʾ laYHWH hāyāh šām ûšᵉmô ʿōḏēḏ — 'a prophet of YHWH was there named Oded' (2 Chronicles 28:9). The narrator's formula introduces one of the most unusual prophetic figures in Chronicles: a northern (Israelite) prophet who intercedes on behalf of southern (Judahite) captives against his own king and army. The hayyāh šām (was there) is a simple presence-marker, but its function is theologically weighty: YHWH's word operated across the political north-south divide. The speech form in 2 Chronicles 28:9-11 uses standard prophetic announcement language but directed at the northern army — your brothers, the wrath of YHWH is upon you. The rarity of a cross-kingdom prophetic intervention in the historical books makes this passage exceptional.
15: wayyilbᵉšûm wayyinᵉʿilûm... wayyaʾᵃkîlûm... wayyašqûm... wayyisᵉkûm... wayyarkîbûm... wayyᵉḇîʾûm liyrîḥôh — the action chain of 2 Chronicles 28:15. The named men execute a sequence of seven compassion-acts toward captured Judahites: clothed-them, sandaled-them (wayyinᵉʿilûm — put sandals on them), fed-them, gave-them-drink, anointed-them (wayyisᵉkûm — anointed with oil, the medical act), carried-on-donkeys the faint, and brought-them to Jericho. The kol-ḥᵃlûšêhem (all their feeble ones) identifies the needy category. The specificity of seven acts and the destination (liyrîḥôh, Jericho) form the closest narrative analogue in the OT to the Good Samaritan parable — the unexpected party acting as comprehensive care-giver to the enemy's vulnerable. The wayyiqtol chain communicates efficient, thorough execution of compassion against all expectation.
22: wayyōsep lim'ōl baYHWH — 'he added to his trespass against YHWH' (2 Chronicles 28:22). The idiom yāsap + mā'al (to add/compound + trespass/unfaithfulness) is the Chronicler's formula for cumulative covenant-breaking. The noun mā'al denotes breach of covenant fidelity — it appears in the Achan story (Josh 7), the priestly trespass laws (Leviticus 5:15), and as the Chronicler's terminal diagnostic for fallen kings (2 Chronicles 36:14). The phrase 'this was that king Ahaz' (hûʾ hammelek ʾāḥāz) functions as an epitaphic marker — a summary verdict that compounds the damning record. The Chronicler's theology of accumulation: unfaithfulness does not plateau; it deepens.
1: ʾāḥāz ben-ʿeśrîm šānāh bimlokô — 'Ahaz was twenty years old when he became king' (2 Chronicles 28:1). The accession formula for Ahaz introduces the most consistently negative reign in the pre-exilic Judean narrative (excluding Manasseh's opening). At twenty, he is young; his sixteen-year reign (735-715 BC) spans the Syro-Ephraimite crisis, the fall of Samaria (722 BC), and the early period of Isaiah's and Micah's ministries. The Chronicler's evaluation is unambiguous: unlike all previous Judean kings (even the qualified ones), Ahaz receives no positive assessment.
2: wayyēlek bədarəkê malḵê yiśrāʾēl wəgam massēkôt ʿāśāh labbəʿālîm — 'He walked in the ways of the kings of Israel and even made cast images for the Baals' (2 Chronicles 28:2). darəkê malḵê yiśrāʾēl (ways of the kings of Israel) — the most damning comparison available to the Chronicler. The northern kingdom's kings are the epitome of covenant apostasy; Ahaz is the first Judean king explicitly compared to them (2 Kings makes a similar but less emphatic comparison, 2 Kings 16:3). The massēkôt labbəʿālîm (cast images for the Baals) is plural — systematic Baal worship infrastructure, not a single transgression.
4: wayyəzabbēaḥ wayyəqaṭṭēr babbāmôt wəʿal-haggəḇāʿôt wətaḥat kol-ʿēṣ raʿănān — 'He sacrificed and burned incense on the high places, on the hills, and under every green tree' (2 Chronicles 28:4). The triple location formula — high places, hills, green trees — is the Deuteronomistic catalog of prohibited Canaanite worship sites (cf. Deuteronomy 12:2; 1 Kings 14:23; 2 Kings 17:10). Ahaz replicates all three simultaneously. The systematic nature of the apostasy (every site, every form) is what distinguishes Ahaz from even bad kings who only partially departed from YHWH.
5: wayyittənēhû YHWH ʾĕlōhāyw bəyaḏ melek ʾărām wayyakkuhû wayyišbû mimmennû šiḇyāh gədôlāh — 'Therefore YHWH his God handed him over to the king of Syria who defeated him and carried off a great number of captives to Damascus' (2 Chronicles 28:5). wayyittənēhû YHWH ʾĕlōhāyw bəyaḏ (YHWH his God gave him into the hand of) — the standard judgment formula; YHWH is explicitly identified as the agent of Judah's military defeat. The phrase ʾĕlōhāyw (his God) is pointed: even the God Ahaz has abandoned remains his covenant God, and it is precisely that relationship that makes the defeat covenantal judgment rather than mere military contingency.
6: wayyahărōg pəqaḥ ben-rəmalyāhû bîhûḏāh mēʾāh wəʿeśrîm ʾelep bəyôm ʾeḥāḏ hakōl bənê-ḥāyil — 'Pekah son of Remaliah killed 120,000 in Judah in a single day — all men of valor — because they had forsaken YHWH, the God of their fathers' (2 Chronicles 28:6). The slaughter of 120,000 valiant men in one day is the largest single-day Judean casualty figure in Chronicles. The Chronicler's explanatory clause — ʿāzəḇû ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇôṯêhem (because they forsook YHWH) — is the theological cause stated after the military fact. The forsaking of YHWH by the people, not just the king, accounts for the scale of judgment.
7: wayyahărōg ziḵrî gibbôr ʾEprayim ʾet-maʿăśêyāhû ben-hammelek wəʾet-ʿazrîqām nəgîḏ habbayit wəʾet-ʾelqānāh mišnēh hammelek — 'Zichri, a warrior of Ephraim, killed Maaseiah the king's son, Azrikam the palace commander, and Elkanah who was second to the king' (2 Chronicles 28:7). The three specifically named officials — ben-hammelek (king's son), nəgîḏ habbayit (palace commander), mišnēh hammelek (second to the king/viceroy) — represent the royal administrative hierarchy. Their deaths in a single battle show the depth of the administrative collapse under Ahaz's apostasy: leadership structure itself is dismantled by the judgment.
8: wayyišbû bənê-yiśrāʾēl mēʾăḥêhem maʾtayim ʾelep nāšîm bānîm ûḇānôt wəgam-šālāl rāḇ bāzəzû mēhem — 'The people of Israel took 200,000 captives from their fellow Israelites — women, sons, and daughters — along with much plunder' (2 Chronicles 28:8). The 200,000 captives from brother Israelites (mēʾăḥêhem — from their brothers) is the theological scandal that drives the Oded episode (vv9-15). Israel-attacking-Judah creates a fratricidal captivity; taking captives from covenant kinsmen violates the fraternal bond. The prophet Oded's speech will exploit precisely this — 'your brothers' — as the moral ground for release.
10: wəʿattāh bənê-yəhûḏāh wîrûšālaim ʾattem ʾōmərîm lirkōš laḵem laʿăḇādîm wəlišfāḥôt mibənê yəhûḏāh wîrûšālaim — 'And now you are planning to make the people of Judah and Jerusalem your male and female slaves — but are you not also guilty before YHWH your God?' (2 Chronicles 28:10). Oded's rhetorical question — halōʾ ʾattem ʿimmākem ʾăšāmôt laYHWH ʾĕlōhêkem — turns the victory's legality against the victors. The guilt (ʾāšam) framework moves the episode from military to cultic: even justified military action creates covenant liability when it crosses the fraternal boundary. This is the Chronicler's unique humanitarianism embedded in covenant theology.
11: wəʿattāh šimʿûnî wəhāšîḇû haššibyāh ʾăšer šəḇîtem mēʾăḥêkem kî ḥărôn ʾap-YHWH ʿălêkem — 'Now hear me, and send back the captives you have taken from your brothers, for the fierce anger of YHWH is upon you' (2 Chronicles 28:11). ḥărôn ʾap-YHWH (YHWH's fierce anger) is the covenant-wrath idiom (cf. Exodus 32:12; Numbers 25:4; Joshua 7:26). Oded frames the brotherly captivity as a sin that has aroused divine wrath against the northern kingdom itself — the conquerors are under judgment, not celebration. The moral calculus reverses the military narrative: Ephraim 'won' but incurred YHWH's wrath by doing so.
12: wayyāqûmû ʾănāšîm mərāʾšê bənê-ʾEprayim — 'Some of the leaders of Ephraim stood up' (2 Chronicles 28:12). The four named leaders (Azariah, Berechiah, Jehizkiah, Amasa) who support Oded represent a prophetic-ethical coalition within the northern military leadership. Their standing against the army — returning captives over the army's objection — is an act of political courage. The Chronicler presents the northern kingdom as capable of moral responsiveness: even apostate Ephraim has leaders who obey a prophetic word. This complicates any simple north-bad/south-good schema.
13: lōʾ-ṯāḇîʾû ʾet-haššibyāh hēnnāh kî ləʾašmat YHWH ʿālênû ʾattem ʾōmərîm lihōsîp ʿal-ḥaṭṭōʾṯênû — 'You must not bring the captives here. You would add guilt against YHWH to our sins and our existing guilt' (2 Chronicles 28:13). ʾašmat YHWH (guilt before YHWH) and ḥaṭṭōʾṯênû (our sins) are the twin terms of the Chronicler's sin-vocabulary. The Ephraimite leaders invoke cumulative guilt: their existing sins plus the additional guilt of the captivity would compound unbearably. This is sophisticated covenant moral reasoning — acknowledging that prior sin increases the liability of each subsequent transgression.
14: wayyaʿăzōḇ heḥāluṣ ʾet-haššibyāh wəʾet-habbizzāh lipnê haśśārîm wəkol-haqāhāl — 'So the armed soldiers released the captives and handed the plunder over to the officials and the whole assembly' (2 Chronicles 28:14). The formal public release before the officials and assembly (kol-haqāhāl) gives the humanitarian act legal standing — it is not a private gesture but an official act witnessed by the community. The assembly's role as witness is the Chronicler's recurring governance principle: significant decisions require public ratification by the covenant community.
16: bāʿēt hahîʾ šālaḥ hammelek ʾāḥāz ʿal-malḵê ʾaššûr laʿăzōr lô — 'At that time King Ahaz sent to the kings of Assyria for help' (2 Chronicles 28:16). The plural malḵê ʾaššûr (kings of Assyria) is unusual — 2 Kings 16:7 has the singular. The Chronicler may reflect a different tradition or acknowledge multiple Assyrian engagements (Tiglath-pileser III and his successors). The seeking of Assyrian help against covenant brothers (after YHWH's explicit judgment) is the theological nadir: instead of seeking YHWH, Ahaz seeks the empire that will ultimately destroy Judah.
17: wəʿôḏ ʾĕdômîm bāʾû wayyakkû bîhûḏāh wayyišbû šiḇyāh — 'For the Edomites had again come and attacked Judah and taken captives' (2 Chronicles 28:17). The Edomite attack is the Chronicler's addition not in 2 Kings; it expands the catalog of enemies that simultaneously assault a weakened Judah. Edom's attack recalls Amaziah's earlier Edomite conquest (2 Chronicles 25:11-12) and its consequences: Amaziah adopted Edomite gods; now Edom attacks Judah under Ahaz. The reversal of Amaziah's victory under Ahaz shows the generational cost of apostasy.
18: ûpəlištîm pāšəṭû bəʿārê haššəpēlāh wəhannegeḇ lîhûḏāh — 'The Philistines had also raided the cities of the Shephelah and the Negeb of Judah' (2 Chronicles 28:18). The simultaneous Edomite (v17) and Philistine (v18) attacks represent the military collapse of the west and south — on top of the Aramean (v5) and Israelite (v6) defeats on the north and east. All four compass points are hostile. This four-directional siege is the Chronicler's spatial representation of total covenant failure: the land that YHWH protected for the faithful is now exposed on every front.
19: kî hiknîaʿ YHWH ʾet-yəhûḏāh baʿăḇûr ʾāḥāz melek yiśrāʾēl kî hipriʿa bîhûḏāh ûmaʿōl maʿal bYHWH — 'For YHWH humbled Judah because of King Ahaz, for he had brought Judah low and had been utterly faithless to YHWH' (2 Chronicles 28:19). maʿal maʿal bYHWH (acted with utter unfaithfulness toward YHWH) — the doubled māʿal (treachery/breach of faith against a superior) is the Chronicler's strongest infidelity term, used for the ultimate covenant violations: Saul's death (1 Chronicles 10:13), the exile of the northern kingdom (1 Chronicles 5:25), and Manasseh's early reign (2 Chronicles 33:19). Calling Ahaz melek yiśrāʾēl (king of Israel) rather than 'king of Judah' is a polemical designation: he behaved like a northern apostate king.
20: wayyāḇōʾ ʿālāyw Tiglat Pilneʾser melek ʾaššûr wayyāṣer lô wəlōʾ ḥăzāqô — 'Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria came to him but oppressed him instead of strengthening him' (2 Chronicles 28:20). The ironic reversal of Ahaz's appeal: he sent for help (laʿăzōr, v16) but received oppression. wayyāṣer lô wəlōʾ ḥăzāqô (oppressed him and did not strengthen him) is the exact anti-fulfillment of the intended purpose. Assyria, the empire Ahaz bought with temple treasures, becomes another oppressor. The Chronicler's point: seeking foreign military power instead of YHWH produces the opposite of the desired outcome.
21: kî ḥālaq ʾāḥāz ʾet-bêt YHWH wəʾet-bêt hammelek wəhaSśārîm wayyittēn lamelek ʾaššûr wəlōʾ laʿăzārāh lô — 'For Ahaz had stripped the house of YHWH, the royal palace, and the officials, and given tribute to the king of Assyria — but it did not help him' (2 Chronicles 28:21). ḥālaq (stripped, took a portion from) followed by triple source (temple + palace + officials) shows systematic despoliation of every sacred and institutional resource. The threefold stripping mirrors the fourfold military attack: total external defeat corresponds to total internal stripping. The Chronicler's refrain — wəlōʾ laʿăzārāh lô (but it did not help him) — is the indictment of the entire Assyrian policy.
23: wayyizabbēaḥ lēʾlōhê Dammāśeq hammakkîm bô wayyōʾmer kî ʾĕlōhê malḵê ʾărām hēm maʿăzərîm ʾôtām lāhem ʾăzabbēaḥ wîʿāzərûnî — 'He sacrificed to the gods of Damascus that had struck him down, reasoning: Since the gods of the kings of Aram help them, I will sacrifice to them so they will help me' (2 Chronicles 28:23). The theological reasoning is nakedly pragmatic — Ahaz treats gods as military assets: whichever deity wins, adopt it. This is the logical extension of his non-seeking of YHWH: gods are tools for outcomes, not persons to whom covenant fidelity is owed. The irony is that the Aramean gods 'struck him' (hammakkîm bô) — worshipping the gods of those who defeated you is the Chronicler's portrait of irreversible spiritual confusion.
24: wayyeʾĕsōp ʾāḥāz ʾet-kəlê bêt-hāʾĕlōhîm wayyəqaṣṣēṣ ʾet-kəlê bêt-hāʾĕlōhîm wayyisgōr ʾet-daltôt bêt-YHWH wayyaʿas-lô mizbəḥôt bəkol-pinnāh bîrûšālaim — 'Ahaz collected the vessels of God's house, smashed them, shut the doors of YHWH's house, and set up altars at every street corner in Jerusalem' (2 Chronicles 28:24). The four actions — collect, smash, shut, set up altars — form the Chronicler's anti-temple narrative. Shutting the temple doors (wayyisgōr ʾet-daltôt bêt-YHWH) is the definitive anti-covenant act: the house of meeting with YHWH is sealed. This closing creates the dramatic setup for Hezekiah's first act as king: opening the temple doors (2 Chronicles 29:3). The father-son contrast is the Chronicler's literary architecture.
25: ûḇəkol-ʿîr wāʿîr bîhûḏāh ʿāśāh bāmôt ləqaṭṭēr lēʾlōhîm ʾăḥērîm wayyakʿes ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇōṯāyw — 'In every city of Judah he set up high places to burn incense to other gods, provoking the LORD, the God of his fathers' (2 Chronicles 28:25). bəkol-ʿîr wāʿîr (in every city) — total geographic saturation of apostasy. Every Judean city now has an official high place; the temple of YHWH is shut while altars to other gods proliferate. wayyakʿes ʾet-YHWH (provoked YHWH) — the anger-of-God formula used primarily for idolatry. The Chronicler reserves this term for Ahaz's reign as the culminating expression of covenantal provocation.
26: wəyeter dəḇārāyw wəkol-darākāyw hārîʾšōnîm wəhāʾaḥărônîm hinnām kəṯûḇîm ʿal-sēper malḵê yəhûḏāh wəyiśrāʾēl — 'The rest of his acts and all his ways, from beginning to end, are written in the Book of the Kings of Judah and Israel' (2 Chronicles 28:26). The archival citation is formulaic but loaded: 'all his ways' (kol-darākāyw) refers back to v2's 'he walked in the ways of the kings of Israel.' His 'ways' are a synonym for apostasy in this context. The citation points to a fuller account of the damage Ahaz did — Chronicles is selective; the fuller record is even worse.
27: wayyiškaḇ ʾāḥāz ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw wayyiqbəruhû bāʿîr bîrûšālaim kî lōʾ hēḇîʾuhû liḵwərôt malḵê yiśrāʾēl — 'Ahaz slept with his ancestors and they buried him in the city of Jerusalem, but they did not bring him to the tombs of the kings of Israel' (2 Chronicles 28:27). The burial note is the Chronicler's final verdict: Ahaz is denied the Davidic royal tomb. He is buried in Jerusalem but not in the tomb of the kings — the same exclusion-in-proximity as Uzziah (2 Chronicles 26:23) but without the specific designation of the leper-burial field. The phrase 'tombs of the kings of Israel' — using 'Israel' for the Davidic line — is the Chronicler's ironic echo: Ahaz who walked in the ways of the kings of Israel (v2) is excluded from the tombs of the kings of Israel (the Davidic line). The two 'Israels' do not overlap for him.
